name: WACT 4.0
purpose: Evaluate enterprise workflow agentification opportunities across Industry × Function combinations. WACT assesses whether a specific workflow is ready for AI agent deployment and how AgentDash should approach it. Evolved from PACT 3.0 with doubled sub-criteria, each grounded in market evidence and AgentDash-specific implications.

dimensions.name: Workability
dimensions.description: How well does this workflow's structure lend itself to being executed by AI agents? Evaluates task complexity, measurability, error tolerance, data quality, existing automation gaps, competitive landscape, and time-to-proof for AgentDash.

subCriteria.name: Task Complexity Profile
subCriteria.description: Is the work linear (extract→send) or non-linear (read→adjust→negotiate)? Linear, rule-driven workflows score higher.

rubric.1: Requires open-ended creative judgment with no clear rules or success criteria
rubric.3: Mix of structured and semi-structured tasks. Core steps follow rules but edge cases need judgment
rubric.5: Predominantly checklist-driven, rule-based SOPs with bounded decision logic

subCriteria.name: Outcome Measurability
subCriteria.description: Can you prove ROI in weeks not months? Workflows with clear, fast feedback loops score higher.

rubric.1: Outcomes are subjective, take months to measure, and lack ground truth
rubric.3: Partially measurable. Some tasks have clear metrics but feedback loops take weeks
rubric.5: Objectively measurable with rapid feedback. Clear metrics: cases closed, time saved, cost reduced

subCriteria.name: Error Tolerance & Reversibility
subCriteria.description: What happens when the agent makes a mistake? Workflows with reversible, bounded errors score higher.

rubric.1: Errors cause irreversible harm: financial loss, patient harm, legal liability
rubric.3: Some actions reversible, others carry moderate consequences. Errors caught within hours/days
rubric.5: Most actions fully reversible or bounded in impact. Natural checkpoints catch errors before they matter

subCriteria.name: Latency Requirements
subCriteria.description: Real-time vs batch tolerance. Batch-friendly workflows are more agent-ready.

rubric.1: Sub-second real-time responses required. Delays directly cause revenue loss or safety issues
rubric.3: Minutes-level response time acceptable. Near-real-time for some paths
rubric.5: Batch/async workflows where hours or days of processing is normal

subCriteria.name: Data Availability & Structure
subCriteria.description: Is the input data clean and structured, or buried in PDFs, handwritten notes, and audio?

rubric.1: Scattered across 10+ formats: scanned docs, handwritten notes, audio, proprietary files
rubric.3: Mix of structured databases and semi-structured docs, emails, chat logs
rubric.5: Primarily structured data with consistent schemas and standard formats

subCriteria.name: Existing Automation Baseline
subCriteria.description: What's already automated (and failing)? Markets where current RPA/rules-based automation hits walls are prime agent targets.

rubric.1: No automation exists. Entirely manual with no digital trail
rubric.3: Basic RPA or rules-based automation exists but breaks on edge cases and unstructured data
rubric.5: Mature automation stack exists but has a clear ceiling that AI agents can break through

subCriteria.name: Current Players & Gaps
subCriteria.description: Who's already building agents for this cell? Where do they fall short? White space = opportunity.

rubric.1: Crowded with well-funded, established competitors delivering strong results
rubric.3: Several players exist but none dominate. Clear gaps in coverage or quality
rubric.5: Wide open white space — no credible agent solution exists despite clear demand

subCriteria.name: Multi-Agent Complexity
subCriteria.description: Does this need a single agent or a coordinated squad? AgentDash's 7-Squad model excels at multi-agent workflows.

rubric.1: Single-purpose agent sufficient. No coordination needed
rubric.3: 2-3 agents need to coordinate with basic handoffs
rubric.5: Complex multi-agent orchestration required — exactly where AgentDash's Squad model shines

subCriteria.name: Benchmark Availability
subCriteria.description: Can we demonstrate superiority objectively? Existing benchmarks or test datasets accelerate proof.

rubric.1: No benchmarks exist. Success is subjective and debatable
rubric.3: Industry KPIs exist (cycle time, accuracy) but no agent-specific benchmarks
rubric.5: Published benchmarks, test datasets, or competitions exist for this exact workflow

subCriteria.name: Time-to-Proof
subCriteria.description: How fast can AgentDash show a working demo? Faster = better for 30-day GTM sprints.

rubric.1: Months of integration and domain-specific training needed before any demo
rubric.3: 2-4 weeks to a credible POC with moderate integration effort
rubric.5: Days to a working demo using existing domain specs and minimal integration

dimensions.name: Access
dimensions.description: Can the agent autonomously navigate the target systems, authenticate, read/write data, and comply with data residency requirements? Evaluates system complexity, API maturity, authentication burden, and AgentDash's integration advantages.

subCriteria.name: System Landscape Complexity
subCriteria.description: How many systems must the agent interact with? Fewer, well-API'd systems score higher.

rubric.1: 8+ heterogeneous systems across multiple vendors, protocols, and data formats
rubric.3: 4-6 systems with documented APIs. Some custom connectors needed
rubric.5: 1-3 well-documented systems with modern REST/GraphQL APIs

subCriteria.name: API Maturity
subCriteria.description: Modern REST/GraphQL vs legacy SOAP/EDI/fax. Modern APIs dramatically reduce integration LOE.

rubric.1: Dominated by legacy: mainframes, SOAP, FTP, proprietary databases, fax workflows
rubric.3: Mix of modern and legacy. Core systems have APIs but some require middleware
rubric.5: Modern cloud-native platforms with comprehensive APIs and webhooks

subCriteria.name: Authentication Burden
subCriteria.description: OAuth-only is easy. VPN + 2FA + certificate chains are hard. Simple auth = faster deployment.

rubric.1: Multi-factor with human ceremonies (SMS codes, hardware tokens), VPN, portal scraping
rubric.3: Standard OAuth/API keys across most systems. Some complexity with token refresh
rubric.5: Simple API keys or OAuth 2.0 for all systems. Service accounts straightforward

subCriteria.name: Read/Write Risk Profile
subCriteria.description: What's at stake when agents write data? Low-risk writes = faster autonomous deployment.

rubric.1: High-stakes writes: financial transactions, medical records, legal filings. Errors are costly and irreversible
rubric.3: Mix of reads and bounded writes. Most writes are reversible or require approval
rubric.5: Primarily read operations with low-risk, bounded, auditable writes

subCriteria.name: Data Sovereignty & Residency
subCriteria.description: HIPAA, FedRAMP, GDPR constraints on data processing, storage, and residency.

rubric.1: Highly regulated data (PHI, classified, PCI) with strict residency and encryption mandates
rubric.3: Moderately sensitive (PII, business-confidential) with standard compliance (GDPR, SOC2)
rubric.5: Primarily non-sensitive or public data. Minimal regulatory constraints

subCriteria.name: Integration Build Effort
subCriteria.description: LOE to connect AgentDash to this cell's system stack. Lower effort = faster GTM.

rubric.1: 6+ months of custom integration work with legacy adapters and complex data mapping
rubric.3: 2-4 weeks of integration using existing APIs and standard connectors
rubric.5: Days — off-the-shelf connectors or industry-standard APIs (FHIR, OCSF, TMF) available

subCriteria.name: Existing Connector Coverage
subCriteria.description: Do platforms in our researched landscape already cover this? We can leverage or leapfrog.

rubric.1: No existing agent platform has connectors for this cell's systems
rubric.3: Some platforms have partial coverage. Gaps in key integrations remain
rubric.5: Multiple platforms have deep connectors — we can learn from their approach and improve

subCriteria.name: Air-Gap Requirement
subCriteria.description: Does this need offline-first or air-gapped operation? AgentDash's architecture uniquely supports this.

rubric.1: Standard cloud SaaS is fine. No air-gap requirement
rubric.3: VPC/private cloud deployment preferred but not mandatory
rubric.5: Air-gapped or classified environment required — AgentDash's unique differentiator

subCriteria.name: Legacy System Advantage
subCriteria.description: Can we reach systems that competitor platforms can't? Legacy access creates moats.

rubric.1: All target systems are modern cloud-native with APIs. No legacy advantage possible
rubric.3: Some legacy systems exist but are accessible via standard middleware
rubric.5: Critical systems are legacy (mainframes, COBOL, proprietary protocols) that only agentic approaches can navigate

subCriteria.name: Partner Ecosystem Leverage
subCriteria.description: Can we ride existing integration partnerships (system integrators, ISVs, channel partners)?

rubric.1: No relevant partners exist. Must build all channel from scratch
rubric.3: Some potential partners exist but no established relationships
rubric.5: Strong partner ecosystem with established channels, co-sell motions, and marketplace presence

dimensions.name: Context
dimensions.description: How effectively can agents ingest, retrieve, and synthesize the enterprise's data without hallucinating? Evaluates knowledge source diversity, RAG requirements, domain model availability, and cross-cell knowledge transfer potential.

subCriteria.name: Knowledge Source Diversity
subCriteria.description: Is knowledge in clean databases or buried in PDFs, Slack, AutoCAD, recorded Zoom calls?

rubric.1: Knowledge scattered across 10+ formats including scanned handwritten docs, audio, proprietary files
rubric.3: Mix of structured DBs and semi-structured docs, emails, chat. Some standardization exists
rubric.5: Primarily structured data with consistent schemas. Standard formats (JSON, CSV, well-formatted PDFs)

subCriteria.name: RAG Precision Requirements
subCriteria.description: How precise must retrieval be? Medical/legal contexts require near-perfect recall vs general support tolerating noise.

rubric.1: Near-perfect recall required. Missing a document could cause legal, medical, or financial harm
rubric.3: Good recall important but some noise tolerable. Most queries have clear answers in corpus
rubric.5: Approximate retrieval sufficient. Agent needs general context, not exact document retrieval

subCriteria.name: Context Volume
subCriteria.description: Does the task need millions of tokens of history or fits in a standard context window?

rubric.1: Requires synthesis of 100+ pages: multi-year case histories, entire regulatory frameworks
rubric.3: 10-50 pages of context: a few policy docs, recent communications, reference materials
rubric.5: Under 10 pages per task. Self-contained decisions fitting in a single LLM call

subCriteria.name: Data Freshness & Volatility
subCriteria.description: How fast does critical data change? Stable reference data is easier than real-time streaming.

rubric.1: Critical data changes in seconds/minutes: live pricing, shipment positions, sensor streams
rubric.3: Daily changes. Morning snapshots mostly valid but some real-time elements
rubric.5: Reference data changes monthly/quarterly/annually. Stable snapshots with periodic refresh

subCriteria.name: Proprietary Knowledge Depth
subCriteria.description: How much tacit, tribal knowledge must agents absorb? Codified policies are easier than expert intuition.

rubric.1: Domain knowledge is primarily tacit, tribal, locked in experts' heads. No published rules
rubric.3: Published grounding sources exist but are incomplete or occasionally contradictory
rubric.5: Comprehensive, authoritative, regularly updated grounding: published regulations, SOPs, checklists

subCriteria.name: Grounding Feasibility
subCriteria.description: How hard is it to build the knowledge base agents need? Existing structured docs = fast. Tribal knowledge = slow.

rubric.1: Would require months of SME interviews and manual knowledge capture. No existing documentation
rubric.3: Documented processes exist but need curation, structuring, and gap-filling. 2-4 weeks of prep
rubric.5: Well-documented domain with published schemas, ontologies, and structured reference data ready to ingest

subCriteria.name: Domain Model Availability
subCriteria.description: Do published industry schemas/ontologies exist (FHIR, OCSF, eTOM, ACORD)? These accelerate agent development.

rubric.1: No industry-standard data models exist. Must create domain ontology from scratch
rubric.3: Some standards exist but adoption is partial. Custom mapping required
rubric.5: Mature, widely-adopted industry standard (FHIR for healthcare, OCSF for security, eTOM for telecom)

subCriteria.name: Training Data Accessibility
subCriteria.description: Can we get real data to improve agents via AgentDash's Darwin Engine? Each deployment should make the system smarter.

rubric.1: Data is locked behind strict access controls with no path to training use. Heavy legal barriers
rubric.3: Data available for training with appropriate agreements and anonymization. Moderate effort
rubric.5: Abundant, accessible training data with clear usage rights. Public datasets supplement proprietary data

subCriteria.name: Hallucination Risk Profile
subCriteria.description: What's the damage from fabricated outputs? Low damage tolerance requires stronger grounding.

rubric.1: Zero tolerance. Fabricated facts cause legal liability, patient harm, or financial loss. Every claim must be verifiable
rubric.3: Low tolerance but with safety nets. Outputs reviewed by humans. Some advisory rather than authoritative
rubric.5: Moderate tolerance. Outputs are reviewed/edited. Used as starting point rather than final answer

subCriteria.name: Cross-Cell Knowledge Transfer
subCriteria.description: Does context built for this cell help adjacent cells? High transfer = compounding returns across the matrix.

rubric.1: Highly specialized knowledge with no applicability to other cells
rubric.3: Some knowledge transfers to adjacent cells in the same industry or function
rubric.5: Strong transfer — domain model, workflows, and grounding data directly accelerate 3+ adjacent cells

dimensions.name: Trust
dimensions.description: When (not if) the agent encounters an edge case, how does it fail safely and transparently? Evaluates regulatory complexity, audit requirements, HITL expectations, and AgentDash's Guardian advantage for building enterprise trust.

subCriteria.name: Regulatory Framework Complexity
subCriteria.description: Overlapping regulations, AI-specific laws, and compliance mandates. More regulation = higher bar but also higher moat.

rubric.1: Multiple overlapping frameworks (HIPAA+state laws, SOX+SEC, GDPR+local privacy). Criminal penalties for non-compliance
rubric.3: Clear regulatory framework with established compliance patterns. SOC2/ISO sufficient
rubric.5: Minimal regulation. Standard business practices apply. No industry-specific AI mandates

subCriteria.name: Audit Trail Requirements
subCriteria.description: Must the agent log transparent chain-of-thought for every action? Higher requirements = AgentDash Guardian advantage.

rubric.1: Legally defensible audit trails with chain-of-custody documentation required for regulatory examination
rubric.3: Moderate audit needs. Key decisions traceable on request. Periodic compliance review
rubric.5: Agent-native logging (chain-of-thought, citations) is actually an improvement over the opaque status quo

subCriteria.name: HITL Model Expectations
subCriteria.description: Pre-approval for every action or exception-only routing? Exception-only = faster autonomous deployment.

rubric.1: Human pre-approval required for virtually every action. HITL burden makes deployment questionable
rubric.3: Tiered: simple cases autonomous, 30-60% need human review. Boundary is definable
rubric.5: Exception-only: agents handle 70%+ autonomously. Humans review only flagged edge cases

subCriteria.name: Explainability Depth
subCriteria.description: Must explain reasoning to regulators, end-users, or just internal teams? Deeper = more differentiation for Guardian.

rubric.1: Full explainability to non-technical audiences: regulators, judges, patients, citizens. Plain language required
rubric.3: Internal stakeholders need reasoning. Some external reporting but not real-time
rubric.5: Minimal explainability. Technical teams interpret results directly. No external reporting

subCriteria.name: Failure Consequence Severity
subCriteria.description: What happens when the agent fails? Financial loss, patient harm, legal liability, or just a support ticket?

rubric.1: Failures cause existential risk: patient harm, criminal liability, national security compromise
rubric.3: Failures cause moderate business impact: lost revenue, customer churn, regulatory scrutiny
rubric.5: Failures cause minor inconvenience: rework, delayed response, support ticket. Low stakes

subCriteria.name: Guardian Advantage
subCriteria.description: Does AgentDash's deterministic policy enforcement (Guardian) create meaningful differentiation in this cell?

rubric.1: Trust requirements are basic — any platform with standard guardrails is sufficient
rubric.3: Guardian's policy enforcement is a nice-to-have that strengthens the sales pitch
rubric.5: Guardian is a hard requirement — deterministic policy enforcement, air-gapped operation, and Rules of Engagement are must-haves that only AgentDash delivers

subCriteria.name: Certification Moat
subCriteria.description: FedRAMP, CMMC, SOC2, HIPAA BAA timelines that block competitors. Each month of certification = a month competitors can't catch up.

rubric.1: No certifications required. Low barrier to entry for competitors
rubric.3: Standard certifications (SOC2) required but achievable in 6-12 months
rubric.5: Heavy certifications (FedRAMP High, CMMC Level 2, IL5) required. 18-24 month competitor delay

subCriteria.name: Trust-Building Speed
subCriteria.description: Can we earn buyer trust within the pilot window? Some industries trust fast, others take years.

rubric.1: Trust requires 2+ years of production history, published case studies, and peer references
rubric.3: 60-90 day pilot can demonstrate trustworthiness if results are strong and transparent
rubric.5: Rapid trust-building: 2-4 week POC with measurable outcomes earns buyer confidence

subCriteria.name: Compliance Automation Potential
subCriteria.description: Can agents self-audit and generate compliance reports? This turns trust from a cost center into a feature.

rubric.1: Compliance is manual, expensive, and requires external auditors for everything
rubric.3: Some compliance can be automated (log collection, report generation) but auditors still needed
rubric.5: Agents can generate compliance-ready audit trails, auto-populate regulatory reports, and self-monitor

subCriteria.name: Buyer Champion Profile
subCriteria.description: Who internally fights for this purchase? What title, what budget, what urgency? Strong champions = faster deals.

rubric.1: No clear buyer champion. Decision diffused across committees with no urgency
rubric.3: Identifiable champion (CIO, VP) with budget authority but competing priorities
rubric.5: Urgent buyer champion (CISO post-breach, CTO under mandate, CFO facing audit) with dedicated budget and board-level pressure
